content.problemStatement:
Chef is feeling thirsty, and decides to make himself something to drink.

He has NNN distinct types of juices with him. There are AiA_iAi​ milliliters of the iii-th juice.

Chef is feeling adventurous, and decides to mix **two different types** of juices to form a single one.

Formally, Chef will do the following:


Choose two distinct types of drinks iii and jjj (1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N)
Then, choose an **integer** xxx between 111 and AiA_iAi​ and an **integer** yyy between 111 and AjA_jAj​; and mix xxx mL of drink iii with yyy mL of drink jjj.
How many different mixes are available for Chef to create?

Note that two mixed drinks are considered different if:


The base drinks used to create the mixes are different (i.e, they have different iii and/or jjj); or
The quantities of base drinks used are different (i.e, they have different xxx and/or yyy)

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of types of juice with Chef.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the quantity of the iii-th drink.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the number of different mixes Chef can create.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 1000 (the sum of N over all test cases ≤ 1000)
The answer fits in a 64‑bit signed integer.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of juice types. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN, where Ai is the amount (in milliliters) of the i‑th juice.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of different mixes Chef can create.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we sum over all unordered pairs (i, j) the product Ai·Aj. The first case: 11·7 = 77. The second case: 1·3 + 1·5 + 3·5 = 3 + 5 + 15 = 23. The third case yields 72, and the fourth case yields 3.5×10^9.

hidden.label: edge case with one small and one large amount

solution.approach: For a fixed unordered pair of distinct juices (i, j) Chef can choose any integer amount from 1..Ai for the i‑th juice and any integer amount from 1..Aj for the j‑th juice. Hence this pair contributes Ai·Aj different mixes. The total number of mixes is therefore Σ_{1≤i<j≤N} Ai·Aj. This can be computed efficiently using the identity Σ_{i<j} Ai·Aj = ((Σ Ai)^2 – Σ Ai^2) / 2. For each test case we compute the sum of all Ai and the sum of their squares in O(N) time, apply the formula, and output the result.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of juice types in that test case.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        sum_vals = 0
        sum_sq = 0
        for _ in range(N):
            a = int(next(it))
            sum_vals += a
            sum_sq += a * a
        ans = (sum_vals * sum_vals - sum_sq) // 2
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
